Aurora & Argo AI.description: Aurora Innovation and Argo AI represent two parallel paths in autonomous driving founded by veterans of Google's Self-Driving Car Project and Carnegie Mellon. Aurora was founded in 2017 by Chris Urmson (ex-Google SDC lead) and focuses on autonomous trucking. Argo AI was founded in 2016 by Bryan Salesky (also ex-Google SDC) with $1B+ backing from Ford and VW, but shut down in 2022 when both automakers withdrew funding. Aurora acquired Uber ATG in 2021 and became the leading pure-play autonomous trucking company.

Luminar, Innoviz, and Aeva pursued automotive OEM partnerships with next-generation architectures.

Mobileye.description: Mobileye was founded in 1999 in Jerusalem, Israel by Amnon Shashua and Ziv Aviram, pioneering vision-based ADAS using custom EyeQ chips. After a high-profile partnership and split with Tesla, Mobileye was acquired by Intel for $15.3 billion in 2017 and re-IPOed in 2022. The company develops both the SuperVision L2+ and Chauffeur L4 autonomous driving systems, alongside the REM crowd-sourced mapping and RSS safety frameworks.

Tesla Autopilot / FSD.description: Tesla entered the autonomous driving space in 2014 through a partnership with Mobileye, rapidly iterating on its Autopilot system after a high-profile split in 2016. Tesla also expanded into AI infrastructure with the Dojo supercomputer and the Optimus humanoid robot program.

Tier IV / Autoware.description: Autoware originated as an open-source autonomous driving project at Nagoya University in 2015, led by Shinpei Kato. Waymo.description: Waymo originated as Google's Self-Driving Car Project in 2009, becoming the longest-running autonomous vehicle program in the industry. It spun off from Google X as an Alphabet subsidiary in 2016 under CEO John Krafcik, later succeeded by co-CEO Tekedra Mawakana and CTO Dmitri Dolgov (now sole CEO). Waymo operates the world's first commercial autonomous ride-hail service, Waymo One, across multiple U.S. cities.
